stem: Under which condition is the boolean expression `a && b` equal to `true`?

options.label: D
options.text: When at least one of `a` or `b` is `false`

explanation: The `&&` (AND) operator returns `true` only when both operands are `true`. Option A describes `||` (OR). Options C and D describe conditions under which `&&` evaluates to `false`.

`score` is 85. The first condition `score >= 90` is false. The second condition `score >= 80 && score < 90` evaluates to `true && true`, which is `true`, so `"B"` is printed and no further branches are evaluated. A student who ignores the upper bound in the second condition might think the third branch fires, choosing C.

Option B short-circuits: if `s != null` is `false`, `s.length()` is never called, preventing a `NullPointerException`. Option A evaluates `s.length()` before the null check, throwing an NPE when `s` is null. Option C fails when `s` is null because `s != null` is `false` and `||` then forces evaluation of `s.length()`, also throwing NPE. Option D evaluates `s.length()` unconditionally as the left operand.

Short-circuit evaluation of `&&` means that when the left operand `x != 0` evaluates to `false` (because `x` is 0), the right operand `y / x` is never evaluated. The division-by-zero never occurs and the `else` branch prints `"small"`. Distractor C would be correct only if Java evaluated both sides of `&&` regardless of the left operand's value.

stem: Which of the following boolean expressions is equivalent to `!(p && q)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's Law, `!(p && q)` equals `!p || !q` — negate each operand AND flip the operator from `&&` to `||`. Option A negates both operands but incorrectly keeps `&&`. Options C and D omit the negation of operands entirely.

`&&` has higher precedence than `||`. Line 1 evaluates as `a || (b && c)` = `true || (false && false)` = `true || false` = `true`. Line 2 evaluates as `(a || b) && c` = `(true || false) && false` = `true && false` = `false`. A student who misremembers precedence and applies `||` before `&&` would incorrectly choose A (both true).

For `r1`: `count++` yields 0 and count becomes 1; `0 > 0` is `false`; `&&` short-circuits so the second `count++` is skipped. For `r2`: `count++` yields 1 and count becomes 2; `1 > 0` is `true`; `||` short-circuits so the second `count++` is skipped. Final `count` is 2. Option C results if only one short-circuit fires; option D results if neither fires.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(x >= 5 && y != 3)`?
explanation: De Morgan's Law states `!(A && B)` = `!A || !B`. Here `!A` = `!(x >= 5)` = `x < 5`, and `!B` = `!(y != 3)` = `y == 3`, giving `x < 5 || y == 3`. Option A uses `&&` instead of flipping to `||`. Option C incorrectly negates `>=` to `<=` rather than `<`. Option D applies neither negation correctly.

`x` is 10, so `x > 5` is `true` and the inner `if` is reached. `x > 15` is `false`, but `x % 2 == 0` is `true` (10 is even). Because `||` only requires one operand to be `true`, the inner condition is `true` and `"yes"` is printed. Distractor B results if a student incorrectly thinks both sides of `||` must be true.

For `n = 15`: `even = false`, `div3 = true`, `div5 = true`. First `if`: `false || (true && true)` = `true` — prints `X`. Second `if`: `!false && (true || true)` = `true && true` = `true` — prints `Y`. Third `if`: `!(true && true)` = `!true` = `false` — `Z` is skipped. Output is `X` then `Y`. Distractor D results if a student incorrectly evaluates `!(div3 && div5)` as `!div3 && !div5` without applying De Morgan's Law, still getting `false`, or simply forgets to negate the entire conjunction.

x > 2 is true (4 > 2) and y < 10 is true (7 < 10), so the && expression is true and 'both' is printed. The else-if and else branches are skipped entirely. D is wrong because only the first true branch executes in an if/else-if/else chain.

Because n is 0, the left operand of && (n != 0) is false. Short-circuit evaluation causes Java to skip evaluating the right operand entirely, so 100 / n is never computed and no ArithmeticException is thrown. The expression evaluates to false and 'false' is printed. C is the classic short-circuit trap distractor.

stem: Which of the following boolean expressions evaluates to `true` when `score` is 75?
explanation: Substituting 75: A is false because 75 > 80 is false, short-circuiting the &&. B is true because 75 >= 70 is true AND 75 <= 80 is true. C is false because both disjuncts are false. D is false because 75 != 75 is false, short-circuiting the &&.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(x > 5 || y == 3)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's Law, !(A || B) = !A && !B. Applying this: !(x > 5) && !(y == 3) simplifies to x <= 5 && y != 3. A incorrectly negates > to < instead of <=. B correctly negates each operand but fails to flip || to &&. D simply removes the outer ! without distributing it.

a % 2 == 0 is false (3 is odd), but b % 2 == 0 is true (6 is even), so the outer || is true and the else branch is skipped. Inside, a < b is true (3 < 6) and b < 10 is true (6 < 10), so the inner && is true and result = "X". B would result if b >= 10 or a >= b; C would result only if both a and b were odd; D is impossible since result is always reassigned.

options.label: C
options.text: An ArithmeticException is thrown at runtime

explanation: Because `x` is 0, `x != 0` evaluates to `false`. Short-circuit evaluation of `&&` means the right operand `y / x > 2` is never evaluated, so no divide-by-zero occurs and the entire condition is `false`, causing the `else` branch to print `not greater`. Option C is the classic trap: students forget that `&&` short-circuits on the first `false` operand.

stem: Which of the following expressions is equivalent to `!(x > 5 && y <= 10)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's Law, `!(a && b)` equals `!a || !b`. Negating `x > 5` gives `x <= 5`; negating `y <= 10` gives `y > 10`; the `&&` flips to `||`. Option A correctly negates both operands but incorrectly keeps `&&`. Option B uses strict inequalities (`x < 5`, `y >= 10`) which are off by one. Option D flips `&&` to `||` but fails to negate either operand.

options.label: D
options.text: The value cannot be determined without knowing the values of `b` and `c`

explanation: `&&` has higher precedence than `||`, so the expression is evaluated as `a || (b && c)`. First, `b && c` = `false && false` = `false`. Then `a || false` = `true || false` = `true`. Option B is the result of incorrectly evaluating left-to-right without respecting precedence: `(a || b) && c` = `true && false` = `false`.

options.label: C
options.text: A NullPointerException is thrown at runtime

options.label: D
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because `s` is assigned `null`

explanation: Since `s` is `null`, `s != null` evaluates to `false`. Short-circuit evaluation of `&&` ensures `s.length() > 0` is never called, so no NullPointerException is thrown. The condition is `false`, so the `else` branch prints `empty or null`. Option C is the common misconception when students forget that `&&` short-circuits on the first `false` operand, which is why the null guard must come first.

First `if`: `5 < 10 && 10 < 15` = `true && true` = `true` → prints X. Second `if`: `5 > 10 || 15 > 10` = `false || true` = `true` → prints Y. Third `if`: `!(5 == 10)` = `!false` = `true` → prints Z. All three conditions are true, producing `XYZ`. Each distractor corresponds to misreading one condition: e.g., option A assumes the second `if` is false by ignoring the `||`.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(score < 70)`?
explanation: Negating `<` yields `>=`: a value that is NOT less than 70 must be greater than or equal to 70. Option B (`score > 70`) is the most common distractor; it incorrectly excludes the boundary case `score == 70`. Options A and D do not represent the correct logical negation of the original inequality.

options.label: C
options.text: Nothing is printed because an `ArithmeticException` is thrown.

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing is printed because the code does not compile.

explanation: Because `x != 0` is false, the `&&` expression short-circuits and `y / x` is never evaluated. Choice A treats the whole condition as true, choice C ignores short-circuiting, and choice D is incorrect because the code compiles.

`&&` is evaluated before `||`, so the first condition is true, the second condition is false, and the third condition is true. Thus `total` becomes `1 + 100`, while the other choices come from misapplying precedence or parentheses.
